title: When to Use Which Metric
concept: Use edit distance for spelling corrections, Jaccard for quick word-set overlap, and Cosine similarity for document matching. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Regular Expressions.'

exercises.prompt: How should you decide which text similarity metric to use?

options.text: Choose based on your specific task goals
Always use Cosine similarity for everything
Flip a coin because they do the same thing

exercises.explain: Choosing the right metric depends on whether you are looking at spelling, shared words, or deep meaning.

cards.title: The Right Tool for the Job
cards.body: We have learned about Edit Distance, Jaccard, and Cosine similarity. But when you are building an app, how do you know which tool to grab from your toolbox? Choosing the wrong one can make your AI slow or inaccurate!

cards.title: Choosing the Right Metric
cards.body:
Different text tasks require different comparison rulers:

• **Edit Distance** measures character-level edits (insert, delete, swap). Great for spelling corrections.
• **Jaccard Similarity** measures the overlap of word sets. Great for checking shared vocabulary.
• **Cosine Similarity** measures the angle between word vectors. Great for understanding the overall meaning and theme of documents.

cards.title: The Room Similarity Analogy
cards.body:
Think of these metrics like measuring how similar two rooms are:

• **Edit Distance** counts how many pieces of furniture you need to move to make the rooms match exactly.
• **Jaccard Similarity** looks at the shared inventory list of items in the rooms, ignoring where they are.
• **Cosine Similarity** looks at the overall style, theme, and color vibe of the rooms, even if one room is much larger.

cards.title: Real-World Matching
cards.body:
Here is how you would use them in the real world:

• Use **Edit Distance** when a user types "pythn" to suggest "python".
• Use **Jaccard** to quickly filter out spam emails that reuse the same set of spammy keywords.
• Use **Cosine Similarity** to recommend articles with similar topics, like finding other sports news stories.

cards.title: Is Newer Always Better?
cards.body: Many beginners think Cosine similarity is always the best because it is the most advanced. Actually, Cosine similarity can be slow and unnecessary for simple tasks. If you just need to fix a user's typo, a simple Edit Distance is much faster and more accurate!

cards.title: Visualizing the Choices
cards.body: Depending on what you want to analyze, choose the metric that looks at the right level of text structure.

left.text: Edit Distance counts spelling edits like 'cat' to 'cats'.

right.text: Cosine Similarity compares semantic themes between documents.

exercises.prompt: Which metric is best suited for building a search bar autocorrect that fixes "reciept" to "receipt"?
exercises.explain: Edit distance calculates the number of edits (insertions, deletions, substitutions) needed to turn one word into another, which is ideal for autocorrect systems.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Jaccard similarity is highly sensitive to the order of words in a sentence.
exercises.explain: Jaccard similarity treats texts as sets of unique words, completely ignoring the order of those words.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing metric for document meaning:
exercises.sentence: To compare the overall theme and meaning of two long documents, you should use [blank] similarity.
exercises.explain: Cosine similarity focuses on the angle (direction) of document vectors, making it perfect for comparing document meanings regardless of length.

exercises.prompt: Which of these descriptions correctly matches the metric to its primary strength?

options.text: Edit Distance handles character changes, Jaccard handles word overlap, Cosine handles semantic meaning.
Cosine handles spelling mistakes, Jaccard handles sentence structure, Edit Distance handles meaning.
Jaccard handles character typos, Edit Distance handles word vectors, Cosine handles sets.

exercises.explain: The first option accurately describes the core strengths of each metric: Edit distance for characters, Jaccard for word sets, and Cosine for overall document themes.

exercises.prompt: You are building a text search engine. Match each metric to its best real-world use case!

options.text: Jaccard similarity to find semantic meaning, Cosine for character edits
Edit distance for document themes, Cosine for character typos
Edit distance for typos, Jaccard for word overlap, Cosine for deep document meaning
Cosine similarity for character edits, Jaccard for sentence structure

exercises.explain: Edit distance tracks spelling fixes, Jaccard handles word overlap, and Cosine captures deep semantic meaning. Next up: Regular Expressions.
